q3: The model kept defaulting to framing difficulty as a timing or information problem — 'you don't know yet' — when what I needed was the acknowledgment that knowing everything still doesn't resolve the choice. It took several rounds of increasingly precise feedback to get it to name the experience of irreducible judgment rather than just describable uncertainty.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about, especially the named alternatives and the honest treatment of difficulty criteria — those align closely with how I think about what makes documentation actually useful versus just accurate. Where it fell a little short is that it didn't quite capture the specific texture I want in difficulty framing: it's not just 'say it's hard,' it's 'locate the hardness in the problem's structure, not in missing context.' The model got closer with the rubric but still needed nudging to land that distinction.
q6: The rubric made it clearer that the model's default mode is to resolve tension rather than name it — it kept wanting to give the reader a decision procedure even when the whole point was that no decision procedure exists. Without the rubric I might have just experienced that as 'the tone is off' without being able to articulate that the model was structurally avoiding the acknowledgment of irreducible judgment.

q3: The ending kept not landing — we spent multiple rounds on the same closing sentence, which tells me the model understood the structural requirements but struggled to execute the specific tone I was after. It kept producing sentences that gestured at the right idea without committing to it concretely.
q5: The rubric captured a lot of what I care about — 'Design Rationale with Named Alternatives' and 'Honest Treatment of Difficulty' are real things I value, even if I didn't articulate them upfront. But it also seemed to make the model a bit mechanical about applying those criteria, like it was checking boxes rather than writing naturally. The validation paragraph ended up being the best thing in the doc, which tracks with the rubric's priority on that — so it did help.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was consistently stronger on structural and conceptual criteria — the rationale sections, the warning placement — than on the closing-register problem, which is more about feel than rules. The rubric couldn't really encode 'this ending walks out of the room,' so the model kept producing technically compliant endings that still didn't stick the landing until I gave very specific direction.

q3: The structure issue — walls of prose versus scannable sections — took a full round of feedback to fix, which surprised me given how fundamental that is for runbook content. The model got the content right pretty early but kept defaulting to essay format when the use case clearly called for something you can navigate at 2am.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about — especially the rejected-alternatives framing and warning placement, which are the things I feel most strongly about. It probably helped the model prioritize those over generic 'good writing' instincts. That said, the structure problem persisted through an extra iteration even with the rubric there, so it's not like the rubric was a silver bullet.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice that the model's default is to nail the content layer before the structure layer — it gets the reasoning right, then needs a nudge to present it in a form that actually serves the reader's context. Without the rubric I might have chalked that up to the specific task, but seeing it laid out as a priority I realize the model consistently treats structure as secondary to prose quality, which isn't always the right call for operational documentation.
